title: Common Entrance vs State and Private Secondary School Exams: What Parents Need to Know
author: Tklesson Team
content:
The National Common Entrance Examination is not the only path to secondary school in Nigeria. Many states run their own entrance examinations for state-owned secondary schools. Many private schools also administer their own entrance tests. Your child may sit for two, three, or even more entrance exams during their final year of primary school. Each exam has its own format, its own syllabus emphasis, and its own admission process. Here is how the different exams compare and how to prepare your child for multiple entrance tests without overwhelming them.

The National Common Entrance Examination

The National Common Entrance is administered by the National Examinations Council and is used for admission into federal unity colleges across Nigeria. It consists of five papers: Mathematics and General Paper, English and General Paper, Quantitative Reasoning, Verbal Reasoning, and General Paper alone. The exam is taken by students across the country, and admission is competitive, especially for popular unity colleges in urban areas.

The Common Entrance is broad in its coverage. It tests the full primary school curriculum across multiple subjects. The inclusion of Quantitative and Verbal Reasoning papers makes it different from most school-based exams. These papers test logical thinking rather than curriculum content, and many students find them unfamiliar. Preparation for the Common Entrance must include specific practice with these reasoning question types.

State Secondary School Entrance Exams

Many Nigerian states conduct their own entrance examinations for admission into state-owned secondary schools. These exams vary by state in format, content, and timing. Some states use an exam that closely mirrors the Common Entrance, testing Mathematics, English, and General Paper. Others include additional subjects or use a different question format. The state exams are typically less competitive than the federal Common Entrance because they serve a smaller candidate pool, but the most desirable state schools in urban areas can still be highly competitive.

The advantage of state exams is that they are often more straightforward than the Common Entrance. They typically do not include Quantitative or Verbal Reasoning sections. The content is drawn directly from the state's primary school curriculum, which your child has been studying for years. The questions are often more predictable, and past papers, where available, provide a reliable guide to what to expect.

State exams also offer a backup option. If your child performs well on the Common Entrance but does not receive a placement at a preferred unity college, a strong performance on a state exam can secure a place at a good state school. The state exam is not a lesser alternative. It is a parallel path that provides additional options.

Private Secondary School Entrance Exams

Private secondary schools typically set their own entrance examinations. These exams vary widely in format and difficulty. Some private schools use exams that are similar to the Common Entrance, testing Mathematics, English, and General Paper. Others include additional components such as an interview, a writing sample, or a test of religious knowledge if the school is faith-based.

The competitive landscape for private schools varies enormously. The most elite private schools may have admission processes as competitive as the top unity colleges. Less well-known private schools may have more flexible admission standards. Research the specific schools you are considering. Talk to parents of current students. Ask about the entrance exam format and the level of competition. The more you know about each school's process, the better you can prepare your child.

Private school exams often have the advantage of smaller candidate pools and more personalized admission processes. The admissions office may be willing to speak with you directly about your child's application. This personal contact is not available in the federal or state systems, where the process is more bureaucratic and impersonal.

How to Prepare for Multiple Exams

The core content tested by all secondary school entrance exams is similar: Mathematics and English are universal. The skills your child develops preparing for the Common Entrance will serve them well on state and private school exams. The preparation is not separate. It is cumulative. Each exam your child prepares for strengthens the skills needed for the others.

Prioritize the exams based on your family's preferences and the exam dates. If the Common Entrance is your primary goal and it comes first, focus preparation on that exam, incorporating Quantitative and Verbal Reasoning practice. After the Common Entrance, shift focus to any state or private school exams that follow. The skills your child has built will transfer, and the additional practice will only strengthen them further.

Be mindful of your child's capacity. Sitting for multiple entrance exams is demanding. Each exam day requires focus, stamina, and emotional energy. Do not overload your child with too many exams. Choose the ones that align with your family's goals and your child's interests. A child who sits for three well-chosen exams and performs well on all of them is better served than a child who sits for six exams and is exhausted and demoralized by the end.

The Bottom Line

The National Common Entrance is one of several paths to secondary school in Nigeria. State exams and private school entrance tests offer additional options, each with its own format and level of competition. The core skills tested, Mathematics and English, are common to all of them. Prepare for the Common Entrance first if it is your primary goal, then apply those same skills to other exams. Choose exams strategically, not exhaustively. The goal is not to take every available test. It is to secure a place at a school where your child will thrive.
